questions.question: Why do dates in Jira sometimes appear one day earlier or later than expected in Notion?

options.text: Because Notion has a known bug in its date calculation.
options.explanation: Notion's date calculation is generally accurate; the discrepancy is due to the source data.

options.text: Because Jira does not provide timezone information for date fields.
options.explanation: Jira date fields lack timezone metadata, causing Notion to interpret the local date differently.

options.text: Because your computer's system clock is set incorrectly.
options.explanation: The issue is specific to the data import format, not the user's local system time.

options.text: Because the sync interval is set to once every 24 hours.
options.explanation: The sync interval affects how often data is pulled, not the calculation of the date itself.

questions.question: What happens if you change the Jira site URL used to create a sync after it has already been set up?

options.text: The sync will automatically update the URL and continue running.
options.explanation: The URL is directly tied to the sync; changing it severs the connection.

options.text: The sync breaks, leading to stale data and a loss of real-time updates.
options.explanation: Since the URL and sync are tightly coupled, altering the URL invalidates the connection.

options.text: The old data is deleted, and the new URL is automatically connected.
options.explanation: Notion does not automatically migrate data when the URL changes.

options.text: The sync pauses until you manually reset the Jira server.
options.explanation: The sync fails immediately due to the invalid URL configuration.

questions.question: How is the sync logic recently improved for select and multi-select Jira fields?

options.text: It ignores any values that were not present in the original import.
options.explanation: The new logic actively prioritizes values currently being used.

options.text: It prioritizes values actively used in imported issues.
options.explanation: This new logic helps ensure that commonly used values are preserved even if other data is missing.

options.text: It converts all select fields to text fields automatically.
options.explanation: The fields remain select fields, but the matching logic has changed.

options.text: It only imports the first option in a multi-select list.
options.explanation: The improvement focuses on value prioritization, not truncation or conversion.

questions.question: What specific permissions must the Jira API token possess for the sync to function without failure?

options.text: Only read-only access to the Jira board.
options.explanation: Read-only access is insufficient; the token needs broader permissions to function.

options.text: Permissions for webhooks and data access.
options.explanation: The token requires specific API permissions to handle webhooks and import data.

options.text: Administrator rights to the Jira server.
options.explanation: Standard API permissions are sufficient; server-level admin rights are not required.

options.text: No permissions are required as Notion connects via OAuth.
options.explanation: A token with appropriate API permissions is explicitly required for the sync to work.

questions.question: When do linked issues across Jira projects get preserved in Notion?

options.text: If at least one of the two projects is selected in the sync setup.
options.explanation: Linked issues are fragile; they require both projects to be included to maintain the relationship.

options.text: If the issues are within the same project.
options.explanation: Linked issues are specifically those crossing project boundaries.

options.text: Only if both projects are selected when you set up the sync.
options.explanation: Both projects must be in the sync scope for the cross-project relationship to be imported.

options.text: Never, as Notion does not support linked issues from Jira.
options.explanation: Notion does support linked issues, but only under the specific condition mentioned above.

questions.question: If a project has disappeared after renaming the project key, what is the recommended troubleshooting step?

options.text: Rename the project key back to its original name.
options.explanation: Renaming back does not resolve the sync logic issue caused by the key change.

options.text: Remove the affected project from the sync and re-import it using the updated key.
options.explanation: This ensures the sync logic uses the new key to locate the project data.

options.text: Delete the project from the Jira server entirely.
options.explanation: You should only remove the project from the Notion sync, not the source Jira project.

options.text: Ignore the issue as it will resolve itself automatically.
options.explanation: Manual intervention is required to correct the sync configuration.

questions.question: Why might Jira statuses appear to not match Notion statuses one-to-one?

options.text: Because Notion uses a different color scheme for statuses.
options.explanation: The mapping is based on logic, not visual styling.

options.text: Because Jira statuses do not have a one-to-one mapping to Notion statuses.
options.explanation: Notion attempts to map statuses intelligently based on wording, rather than forcing a strict 1:1 match.

options.text: Because the sync feature is currently disabled for statuses.
options.explanation: Status mapping is an active feature designed to translate Jira terminology to Notion.

options.text: Because Notion does not support status fields at all.
options.explanation: Notion fully supports status fields and attempts to map them appropriately.

questions.question: What is the first step to take if you are unable to add or remove projects and properties in a synced database?

options.text: Contact your Notion workspace administrator.
options.explanation: The issue is likely permission-related, but you should check your own access first.

options.text: Ensure you have 'Full access' or 'Can edit' access to the synced database.
options.explanation: These permission levels are required to modify the structure of a synced database.

options.text: Delete the database and start over.
options.explanation: This is a drastic step that isn't necessary if the issue is a permission setting.

options.text: Refresh the page to reload the permissions.
options.explanation: Refreshing does not change your actual permission level within the database.

questions.question: What is the cause of missing field values for select and multi-select types in an existing sync?

options.text: The sync is running too slowly to import large lists.
options.explanation: Speed is not the cause; the logic for which values are prioritized has changed.

options.text: The fields are not supported by the current version of the sync feature.
options.explanation: Select and multi-select fields are fully supported, just with updated logic.

options.text: It is a result of the recent improvement in sync logic prioritizing actively used values.
options.explanation: Older data might not match the new logic, so removing and re-importing helps apply the update.

options.text: The Jira field types have been deprecated by Atlassian.
options.explanation: Atlassian has not deprecated these field types.

questions.question: If you are not seeing updates in Notion, what is the simplest troubleshooting action to try?

options.text: Delete and recreate the sync configuration.
options.explanation: This is unnecessary if a simple refresh fixes the visibility issue.

options.text: Refresh the page.
options.explanation: A page refresh is often the quickest way to force the UI to pull the latest data.

options.text: Check your internet connection.
options.explanation: While connectivity is important, the article specifically suggests refreshing the page first.

options.text: Restart the Notion desktop application.
options.explanation: A page refresh is sufficient and less disruptive than restarting the app.
